message:
chore(beads): close dl59 pajek follow-up + track generator-parity tasks

Flushes the local beads state to JSONL after the pajek NodeView follow-up
and native-generator tasks completed:

  * franken_networkx-dl59 (Pajek round-trip NodeView.get parity) — closed
    by the NodeView.get() addition + pajek round-trip test.
  * franken_networkx-371 (Verify NX parity for DEFAULT constants via
    oracle comparison) — closed; a Rust cargo test asserts all nine
    algorithm defaults match NX exactly so drift is caught at CI.
  * Updates metadata/timestamps on related generator-parity issues.

No code or doc change in this commit — just `br sync --flush-only`
output committed to keep the JSONL ledger aligned with the SQLite DB.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -44,6 +44,7 @@
 {"id":"franken_networkx-371","title":"Verify NX parity for all DEFAULT constants via oracle comparison","description":"## What

Verify that every hardcoded default constant in fnx-algorithms matches the
corresponding NetworkX default EXACTLY. Create a conformance test that:

1. Imports NX and reads its defaults:
   - nx.pagerank defaults: alpha=0.85, max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.katz_centrality defaults: alpha=0.1, beta=1.0, max_iter=1000, tol=1.0e-06
   - nx.hits defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-08
   - nx.eigenvector_centrality defaults: max_iter=100, tol=1.0e-06

2. Calls both NX and FNX with a test graph using defaults
3. Asserts outputs match within tolerance

## Why

Constants that DRIFT from NX defaults silently break parity. This test
catches drift at CI time rather than in production.

## Current State

We believe all defaults match NX, but this is untested. The constants were
set by reading NX source code, not by automated verification.

## Files To Create/Change

- tests/python/test_default_parity.py (new test file)

## Dependencies

Depends on constant documentation being complete (need to know what to test).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:40.507246735Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:27:16.804677930Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:27:16.804050793Z","close_reason":"Added Rust test default_constants_match_networkx that asserts all 9 algorithm defaults match NX exactly. Any drift will be caught by cargo test.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-371","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-hl4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:46.602819373Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-3r3","title":"Structural Decomposition: k-truss, k-factor, k-components, onion layers","description":"## Scope
Implement 4 structural decomposition algorithms:
- k_truss(G, k): maximal subgraph where every edge participates in at least k-2 triangles. Implementation: iteratively remove edges with support < k-2 until convergence. O(m * sqrt(m)) with triangle listing.
- k_factor(G, k): k-regular spanning subgraph. Uses existing matching infrastructure — find k disjoint perfect matchings or Tutte's theorem. Raise NetworkXUnfeasible if no k-factor exists.
- k_components(G): dictionary mapping k to list of k-connected components. Build hierarchy: start with connected components (k=1), refine by testing higher connectivity.
- onion_layers(G): generalized k-core decomposition returning layer assignment. Each layer is the set of nodes removed when peeling the current minimum-degree layer.

## Performance Targets
- k_truss: O(m * sqrt(m)) using triangle listing
- k_components: O(V * max_flow_calls)
- onion_layers: O(V + E) same as k-core

## Test Requirements
- k_truss: verify every edge has >= k-2 triangles, verify maximal (no edges can be added)
- k_factor: verify k-regular, verify spanning
- k_components: verify components satisfy connectivity
- onion_layers: verify layer assignment consistency
- Cross-validate against NetworkX on small graphs (K5, Petersen, path, cycle)","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T02:19:47.714536992Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T23:47:40.609037367Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T23:47:40.608368401Z","close_reason":"Implemented k_truss, onion_layers.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","tier-3"]}
@@ -91,6 +92,7 @@
 {"id":"franken_networkx-6yr","title":"Generators: Growth models (gnc, gnr, directed config, joint degree)","description":"## Scope
Implement 8 growth-model random graph generators:
- gnc_graph(n, seed=None) — growing network with copying. At each step, add node and connect to a random existing node's neighbor (copying mechanism). Produces scale-free degree distributions.
- gnr_graph(n, p, seed=None) — growing network with redirection. New node connects to random node; with probability p, redirect to that node's predecessor.
- fast_gnp_random_graph(n, p, seed=None, directed=False) — O(n+m) Erdős-Rényi using geometric distribution for edge gaps. Much faster than O(n^2) for sparse graphs (p << 1).
- directed_configuration_model(in_degree_sequence, out_degree_sequence, seed=None) — create directed multigraph from separate in/out degree sequences. Pair stubs randomly.
- directed_joint_degree_graph(joint_degrees, seed=None) — directed graph from joint degree dictionary {(d_in, d_out): count}.
- joint_degree_graph(joint_degrees, seed=None) — undirected graph from joint degree dictionary {(d1, d2): count}.
- expected_degree_graph(w, seed=None, selfloops=True) — Chung-Lu model. Edge (i,j) with probability w[i]*w[j]/sum(w). O(n+m) expected.
- directed_havel_hakimi_graph(in_deg_sequence, out_deg_sequence) — deterministic directed graph from in/out degree sequences via Havel-Hakimi.

## Implementation Strategy
- gnc/gnr: sequential growth, O(n) per step, pure Python
- fast_gnp: skip non-edges using geometric distribution, Rust implementation preferred for O(n+m)
- configuration models: pair stubs randomly, may produce self-loops/multi-edges
- Chung-Lu: probabilistic edge placement from weight vector
- Havel-Hakimi: deterministic greedy algorithm

## Test Requirements
- gnc/gnr: verify node count, verify connected (gnc is always connected)
- fast_gnp: verify same statistics as gnp_random_graph with same seed
- configuration_model: in-degree and out-degree sequences match input
- joint_degree: joint degree dictionary of output matches input
- expected_degree: average degree close to expected values
- Reproducibility: same seed → same graph for all generators
- NX cross-validation on small instances","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:38.438280624Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T01:07:01.452975244Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T01:07:01.452161524Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["generators"]}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-6z8n","title":"QUALITY: Comprehensive E2E integration test script with structured logging","description":"## Gap
No end-to-end integration test exercises the FULL user workflow. There are 1389 Python unit tests but no script that:
1. Imports as standalone library and runs realistic workflows
2. Verifies NetworkX backend dispatch works
3. Tests all graph types (Graph, DiGraph, MultiGraph, MultiDiGraph)
4. Runs algorithms on large graphs and checks performance
5. Tests serialization round-trips for all I/O formats
6. Produces structured JSON-line logging for CI parsing

## What to test end-to-end

### Test 1: Standalone library workflow
- Create Graph with 1000 nodes from various generators
- Run shortest_path, pagerank, connected_components, community detection
- Verify results are deterministic (run twice, compare)
- Serialize to JSON, deserialize, verify round-trip
- Measure wall-clock time for each algorithm

### Test 2: NetworkX backend workflow
- Set backend_priority to franken_networkx
- Run standard NetworkX tutorial code (path_graph, shortest_path, pagerank)
- Verify all operations succeed and produce correct results
- Verify no _to_nx fallback occurs for core algorithms (check no NX import during execution)

### Test 3: DiGraph workflow
- Create DiGraph with directed algorithms
- Topological sort, strongly connected components, condensation, transitive closure
- Verify against NetworkX reference

### Test 4: MultiGraph workflow
- Create MultiGraph with parallel edges
- Verify edge key handling, attribute preservation
- Run algorithms that support multigraphs

### Test 5: Large graph stress test
- Generate BA graph with 100K nodes
- Run pagerank, betweenness_centrality (sample)
- Verify completes within 60 seconds
- Check memory usage stays reasonable

### Test 6: Serialization round-trip
- For each I/O format (edgelist, adjlist, GML, GraphML, JSON):
  - Write graph → read back → compare nodes/edges/attrs
  - Verify round-trip preserves all data

## Implementation
Create scripts/e2e_integration_test.py:
```python
import json, time, sys, logging

logging.basicConfig(format='{"ts":"%(asctime)s","level":"%(levelname)s","test":"%(name)s","msg":"%(message)s"}', level=logging.INFO)

def run_test(name, fn):
    log = logging.getLogger(name)
    log.info('START')
    t0 = time.monotonic()
    try:
        fn()
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
        log.info(f'PASS ({elapsed:.3f}s)')
        return True
    except Exception as e:
        elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0
        log.error(f'FAIL ({elapsed:.3f}s): {e}')
        return False
```

## Integration with existing CI
ci.yml already exists (.github/workflows/ci.yml). Add a new job after g4-python-tests:
```yaml
  g4b-e2e:
    name: G4b E2E integration
    needs: [g4-python-tests]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: '3.12' }
      - run: pip install maturin pytest networkx numpy scipy
      - run: maturin develop --features pyo3/abi3-py310
      - run: python scripts/e2e_integration_test.py
```

## Testing requirements
- Script runs in < 3 minutes
- All 6 test scenarios pass
- Structured JSON-line log output parseable by CI
- Exit code 0 if all pass, non-zero if any fail
- Each test reports timing

## Files to create
- scripts/e2e_integration_test.py: E2E test runner
- .github/workflows/ci.yml: add G4b step (modify existing)

## Acceptance criteria
- E2E script runs green on clean maturin develop install
- Covers standalone, backend, DiGraph, MultiGraph, stress, serialization
- Structured logging with timing data per test
- Added to CI pipeline as post-unit-test step","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:50:16.088195270Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T19:00:32.848524726Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T19:00:32.848140950Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"franken_networkx-6z8n","depends_on_id":"franken_networkx-zrlb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:50:45.555296597Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-7b0e","title":"NX-REPLACE Misc Tier-2: minimum_cycle_basis, equitable_color, chromatic_polynomial, chordless_cycles (4 fns)","description":"## Functions (4) — complex algorithms
1. minimum_cycle_basis (__init__.py:6160) — Horton's algorithm: O(V*E^2). Find shortest independent cycles.
2. equitable_color (__init__.py:6800) — graph coloring where each color is used roughly equally
3. chromatic_polynomial (__init__.py:6808) — deletion-contraction recursion (exponential worst case)
4. chordless_cycles (__init__.py:6169) — enumerate all chordless cycles

## Implementation approach
- minimum_cycle_basis: Horton's algorithm uses all-pairs shortest paths (have in Rust) + Gaussian elimination on GF(2) cycle space. Medium-hard.
- equitable_color: Welsh-Powell + rebalancing. Builds on existing greedy_color.
- chromatic_polynomial: deletion-contraction with memoization. Exponential but correct.
- chordless_cycles: DFS-based enumeration with chordality check.

## Testing requirements
Create tests/python/test_misc_tier2_parity.py:
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_triangle: verify MCB of K3 is single triangle
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_grid: verify on 3x3 grid matches NX
- test_minimum_cycle_basis_weight: verify weighted MCB matches NX
- test_equitable_color_valid: verify coloring is valid and equitable (max-min color count <= 1)
- test_equitable_color_matches_nx: compare on C7, Petersen
- test_chromatic_polynomial_k4: verify χ(K4, k) = k(k-1)(k-2)(k-3)
- test_chordless_cycles_matches_nx: compare cycle sets on small graphs

## Files to modify
- crates/fnx-algorithms/src/lib.rs: implement 4 algorithms
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs: add bindings
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py: rewire
- tests/python/test_misc_tier2_parity.py: new test file","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-03T02:42:45.416214917Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T03:42:29.288996241Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T03:42:29.288627383Z","close_reason":"Replaced minimum_cycle_basis, chordless_cycles, equitable_color, and chromatic_polynomial delegations with local implementations and added no-delegation parity coverage","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-bvz","title":"Review dispatch risk_probability hardcoded values","description":"## What

The fnx-dispatch and fnx-readwrite crates pass hardcoded risk_probability values
in DispatchRequest structs throughout the code. Examples:

- fnx-readwrite: risk_probability: 0.03 (edgelist write), 0.09 (json read)
- fnx-dispatch test: risk_probability: 0.01, 0.2, 0.3

These values feed into the decision_theoretic_action() function which determines
whether an operation is allowed in strict vs hardened mode.

## Assessment

The risk_probability values are NOT arbitrary magic numbers — they represent
calibrated estimates of the probability that an operation introduces
incompatibility with NetworkX. They are:

- 0.01-0.03: well-tested, low-risk operations (edgelist write)
- 0.08-0.09: moderate operations with known edge cases (json parse, generators)
- 0.2-0.5: higher-risk operations (format conversion, dispatch routing)

## What To Consider

1. Should these be configurable per-mode? (strict might want lower thresholds)
2. Should they be documented as calibration parameters?
3. Are the current values justified by evidence? (conformance test coverage)
4. The test at line 354 uses 0.2 then was changed to 0.3 — why?

## Recommended Action

Add a comment block at the top of fnx-readwrite documenting the risk calibration
methodology. Don't change the values unless conformance evidence supports it.

## Files To Review

- crates/fnx-readwrite/src/lib.rs (all DispatchRequest sites)
- crates/fnx-dispatch/src/lib.rs (decision_theoretic_action thresholds)
- crates/fnx-python/src/algorithms.rs (generator risk_probability values)","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:07:55.645980347Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:23:34.406675540Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:23:34.406070695Z","close_reason":"Added calibration documentation at top of fnx-readwrite/src/lib.rs explaining all risk_probability tiers (0.03/0.08/0.09) with justification. Values are correct and calibrated.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-deh","title":"Drawing & Visualization: draw_networkx variants, LaTeX export, network_text","description":"## Scope
Implement ~15 drawing/visualization functions:
- `draw_networkx` — main drawing function (matplotlib)
- `draw_networkx_nodes` — draw nodes only
- `draw_networkx_edges` — draw edges only
- `draw_networkx_labels` — draw node labels
- `draw_networkx_edge_labels` — draw edge labels
- `draw_circular` — draw with circular layout
- `draw_kamada_kawai` — draw with Kamada-Kawai layout
- `draw_planar` — draw with planar layout
- `draw_random` — draw with random positions
- `draw_shell` — draw with shell layout
- `draw_spectral` — draw with spectral layout
- `draw_spring` — draw with spring layout
- `draw_forceatlas2` — draw with ForceAtlas2 layout
- `write_latex` / `to_latex` / `to_latex_raw` — export graph to LaTeX/TikZ
- `network_text` — ASCII art representation of graph

## Implementation Approach
- Most draw_* functions delegate to matplotlib with layout computation
- Can wrap NetworkX drawing directly for many of these
- network_text: ASCII tree/graph rendering, pure Python
- LaTeX export: generate TikZ code, pure Python string generation

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/`

 {"id":"franken_networkx-do1","title":"Traversal Extras: bfs_beam_edges, bfs/dfs_labeled_edges, generic_bfs_edges","description":"## Scope
Implement 4 advanced traversal functions:
- bfs_beam_edges(G, source, value, width=None) — BFS with beam search pruning. At each level, only expand the top-width nodes as ranked by value(node). Returns edges of the beam-search tree. Useful for exploring large graphs with a heuristic.
- bfs_labeled_edges(G, source, sort_neighbors=None) — yield (u, v, label) where label is one of 'tree' (tree edge in BFS tree), 'level' (edge to node at same level), 'forward' (edge to already-visited node at deeper level), 'reverse' (back edge). Uses BFS with level tracking.
- dfs_labeled_edges(G, source=None, depth_limit=None) — yield (u, v, label) where label is 'tree' (DFS tree edge), 'forward' (edge to descendant not via tree), 'back' (edge to ancestor), 'cross' (edge to node in different subtree), 'nontree' (any non-tree edge). Standard DFS classification.
- generic_bfs_edges(G, source, neighbors=None, depth_limit=None, sort_neighbors=None) — BFS with customizable neighbor function. neighbors(node) returns iterable of adjacent nodes, allowing filtered/weighted BFS.

## Implementation Strategy
- All are generators (yield edges as discovered)
- bfs_labeled: track discovery level per node, classify edges by level comparison
- dfs_labeled: track discovery/finish times, classify by back/forward/cross rules
- generic_bfs: same as bfs_edges but calls neighbors(node) instead of G.neighbors(node)
- bfs_beam: at each level, sort candidates by value(), keep top width

## Test Requirements
- Tree edges form a valid spanning tree (connected, n-1 edges)
- Back edges in DFS create cycles
- Forward edges go to deeper nodes, cross edges go to different subtrees
- bfs_beam with width=None equals regular BFS
- generic_bfs with default neighbors equals regular BFS
- NX cross-validation: same edge classifications on small directed graphs","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:37.942681144Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T22:38:06.367225188Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T22:38:06.366727547Z","close_reason":"Implemented 4 traversal functions: bfs_beam_edges, bfs_labeled_edges, dfs_labeled_edges, generic_bfs_edges.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","traversal"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-e21","title":"Extract scale_free_graph parameter sum epsilon to named constant","description":"## What

Replace inline 1e-6 in fnx-generators scale_free_graph alpha+beta+gamma validation
with the named constant PARAM_SUM_EPSILON (defined as 1.0e-6).

## Status: COMPLETED (March 2026)

Line ~1214 in fnx-generators/src/lib.rs:
  Before: if (sum - 1.0).abs() > 1e-6
  After:  if (sum - 1.0).abs() > PARAM_SUM_EPSILON

## Why 1e-6 (not tighter)

This validates user-supplied parameters (alpha + beta + gamma = 1.0). Users pass
f64 values like 0.41 + 0.54 + 0.05 which may not sum to exactly 1.0 due to
representation error. 1e-6 is loose enough to accept reasonable inputs but tight
enough to catch obviously wrong parameters (e.g., forgetting to include gamma).

NetworkX uses the same tolerance: abs(alpha + beta + gamma - 1.0) >= 1e-6.

## Files Changed

- crates/fnx-generators/src/lib.rs (lines 17, ~1214)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:56.851146350Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:02.646456488Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:02.645818911Z","close_reason":"Inline 1e-6 replaced with PARAM_SUM_EPSILON constant. Verified.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-e3ri","title":"Generator parity follow-up: exact public random_unlabeled_rooted_tree API and seed adapters","description":"## Scope
This rooted-forest port added exact private rooted-tree helpers, but the public `random_unlabeled_rooted_tree` surface still does not match NetworkX semantics. Seed handling also only covers stdlib-style seeds for the new forest path.

## Goal
Bring the public rooted-tree API to parity and decide whether to mirror NetworkX's broader seed adapter behavior (notably `number_of_trees`, null-tree contract, and NumPy-backed random-state inputs).

## Files
- python/franken_networkx/__init__.py
- tests/python/test_generator_delegations_parity.py
- potentially additional generator parity tests","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T14:52:19.369182970Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T16:00:17.075403732Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T16:00:17.075140474Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-l85","title":"Layout Algorithms: force-directed, bipartite, BFS, spiral layouts","description":"## Scope
Implement 8 graph layout functions:
- `arf_layout` — Attractive-Repulsive Force layout
- `bfs_layout` — BFS-based hierarchical layout
- `bipartite_layout` — position bipartite graph in two columns/rows
- `forceatlas2_layout` — ForceAtlas2 algorithm (popular in Gephi)
- `fruchterman_reingold_layout` — Fruchterman-Reingold force-directed (spring_layout)
- `multipartite_layout` — position multipartite graph in columns
- `rescale_layout_dict` — rescale positions to fit in a bounding box
- `spiral_layout` — position nodes along a spiral

## Implementation Approach
- Most can be implemented in Python with numpy for vector operations
- ForceAtlas2: iterative force simulation. Can optionally use Barnes-Hut approximation for O(V log V) per iteration.
- Fruchterman-Reingold: standard spring-electrical model. Already common, may delegate to NetworkX.
- bipartite_layout / multipartite_layout: simple coordinate assignment based on partition membership.
- BFS layout: compute BFS layers, assign x by layer, y by position in layer.
- spiral_layout: parametric curve, assign nodes along Archimedean spiral.
- arf_layout: similar to force-directed but with specific attractive-repulsive function.
- rescale_layout_dict: simple affine transformation.

## Implementation Location
- Python: `python/franken_networkx/drawing/layout.py`
- No Rust needed (layout is not performance-critical for typical graph sizes)

## Test Requirements
- Verify output is dict of {node: (x, y)} pairs
- Verify no overlapping positions for reasonable graphs
- Verify bipartite_layout places nodes in correct partitions
- Cross-validate against NetworkX layouts","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:13:12.197932948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T03:18:43.081547908Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T03:18:43.081005561Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["layout","tier-3"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-rbda","title":"DOCS: First-time user experience — quickstart, examples, API reference","description":"## Gap
README.md has a Quick Start section but the user experience for someone discovering the library is thin:
- No expanded API reference (just the algorithm table in README)
- No migration guide from NetworkX
- No performance comparison data visible to users
- No contributing guide
- No doctest verification that examples actually work

Note: PyPI publication (ciq6) and CI (zrlb) are now COMPLETE. Release workflow and E2E tests exist. This bead focuses on user-facing documentation.

## What to create

### 1. docs/ directory structure
- docs/quickstart.md: expanded tutorial with real-world graph analysis examples
  - Social network analysis (build graph, find communities, compute centrality)
  - Road network shortest paths (weighted Dijkstra)
  - Biological network (bipartite, clustering)
- docs/migration.md: 'switching from NetworkX' guide
  - Side-by-side code comparisons for top 20 patterns
  - What's identical, what differs, known limitations
  - Backend mode (zero changes) vs standalone mode
- docs/algorithms.md: algorithm reference with complexity and examples
  - Grouped by family (shortest path, centrality, community, etc.)
  - Each entry: signature, complexity, example, NX equivalent
- docs/performance.md: benchmark comparison vs NetworkX
  - Pagerank: 20x speedup chart
  - Shortest path: comparison data
  - Community detection: comparison data
  - Memory usage comparison
- docs/backend.md: NetworkX backend integration guide
  - Setup, configuration, which algorithms dispatch to Rust
  - Which algorithms still fall back to NX and why
- docs/contributing.md: developer guide
  - How to build (maturin develop)
  - How to run tests (pytest, cargo test)
  - Code structure (11 crates, Python wrapper)
  - How to add a new algorithm

### 2. Example scripts
- examples/basic_usage.py: standalone quickstart
- examples/backend_mode.py: using as NX backend
- examples/social_network.py: real-world analysis
- examples/benchmark_comparison.py: reproduce perf claims

### 3. PyPI metadata enhancement
- pyproject.toml: update long_description, classifiers, URLs
  - project-urls: Documentation (link to docs/), Repository (GitHub), Changelog
  - classifiers for Topic::Scientific, Status::Production, Framework::NetworkX

### 4. Doctest verification
- Create scripts/verify_docs.py that extracts code blocks from .md files and runs them
- Add to CI as a documentation gate

## Testing requirements
- Every code example in docs/ must be runnable and produce claimed output
- scripts/verify_docs.py: extract and run all Python code blocks from docs/*.md
- Verify all links in README/docs are valid (no dead links)
- Verify pyproject.toml metadata renders correctly on PyPI

## Files to create
- docs/quickstart.md, migration.md, algorithms.md, performance.md, backend.md, contributing.md
- examples/basic_usage.py, backend_mode.py, social_network.py, benchmark_comparison.py
- scripts/verify_docs.py: documentation verifier
- Update pyproject.toml metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- New user can follow quickstart to working code in < 5 minutes
- Migration guide covers top 20 NetworkX patterns with before/after
- All code examples verified runnable via scripts/verify_docs.py
- PyPI page shows rich metadata with description, links, classifiers","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:50:34.163105605Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-07T02:03:28.510857373Z","closed_at":"2026-04-07T02:03:28.510596129Z","close_reason":"Added first-time-user docs set, runnable examples, docs verifier, CI docs gate, and richer PyPI metadata","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"franken_networkx-req","title":"Isomorphism VF2++: is_isomorphic, isomorphism mapping, all isomorphisms","description":"## Scope
Implement 3 VF2++ isomorphism functions:
- `vf2pp_is_isomorphic` — test if two graphs are isomorphic using VF2++
- `vf2pp_isomorphism` — find one isomorphism mapping
- `vf2pp_all_isomorphisms` — generate all isomorphism mappings

## Algorithmic Approach
VF2++ is an improved version of VF2 with better pruning:
1. Preprocessing: compute node ordering based on degree and label frequency
2. Cutting rules: feasibility checks based on degree, neighborhood, and label constraints
3. Matching: backtracking search with the improved ordering and pruning

Key improvements over VF2:
- Better node ordering reduces search space
- Stronger feasibility checks
- Handles labeled graphs efficiently

## Implementation Location
- **Rust required** for performance: `crates/fnx-algorithms/src/isomorphism/vf2pp.rs`
- Python bindings: `python/franken_networkx/algorithms/isomorphism/`

## Complexity
- Worst case: O(V! * V) but VF2++ has much better practical performance
- Average case on random graphs: near-polynomial

## Edge Cases
- Graphs of different sizes (trivially non-isomorphic)
- Graphs with same degree sequence but non-isomorphic
- Directed vs undirected graphs
- Graphs with node/edge attributes (attribute-constrained isomorphism)
- Self-loops
- Multigraphs

## Test Requirements
- Test on known isomorphic/non-isomorphic pairs
- Petersen graph isomorphisms
- Random graph isomorphism (permute nodes, verify detection)
- Performance benchmarks vs naive VF2
- Stress tests on Miyazaki graphs (hard instances)","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T19:12:59.203156265Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:00.854116699Z","closed_at":"2026-03-23T07:35:00.853461529Z","close_reason":"Implemented 3 VF2++ isomorphism: vf2pp_is_isomorphic, vf2pp_isomorphism, vf2pp_all_isomorphisms.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["algorithms","isomorphism","tier-3"]}

 {"id":"franken_networkx-u6t","title":"Conversion Extras: pandas adjacency, Prüfer, nested tuple, cytoscape, attr_sparse","description":"## Scope
Implement 10 conversion/serialization functions:
- from_pandas_adjacency(df, create_using=None) — build graph from pandas DataFrame adjacency matrix (rows/cols are nodes, values are edge weights)
- to_pandas_adjacency(G, nodelist=None, dtype=None, weight='weight') — export adjacency as DataFrame
- from_prufer_sequence(sequence) — reconstruct labeled tree from Prüfer sequence. Inverse of to_prufer_sequence. O(n) algorithm.
- to_prufer_sequence(T) — extract Prüfer sequence from labeled tree. Iteratively remove minimum-label leaf. O(n log n).
- from_nested_tuple(sequence, sensible_relabeling=False) — build tree from nested tuple representation (((),), ((),()))
- to_nested_tuple(T, root) — convert rooted tree to nested tuple
- cytoscape_data(G, attrs=None) — export graph to Cytoscape.js JSON format (elements array with nodes/edges)
- cytoscape_graph(data, attrs=None) — build graph from Cytoscape.js JSON
- attr_sparse_matrix(G, edge_attr=None, node_attr=None, normalized=False, rc_order=None, dtype=None) — like attr_matrix but returns scipy.sparse
- to_networkx_graph(data, create_using=None) — universal converter from various formats (dict, list, numpy, scipy, pandas) to graph

## Implementation Strategy
- pandas functions: pure Python using pandas DataFrame operations
- Prüfer: O(n) decode via degree counting, O(n log n) encode via leaf removal
- nested_tuple: recursive tree traversal
- Cytoscape: JSON serialization matching Cytoscape.js element spec
- attr_sparse_matrix: compose over existing to_scipy_sparse_array with attribute extraction
- to_networkx_graph: dispatcher checking input type (dict of dicts → from_dict_of_dicts, numpy → from_numpy_array, etc.)

## Test Requirements
- Round-trip: from_X(to_X(G)) must preserve graph structure for all pairs
- Prüfer: sequence length = n-2 for n-node tree, decode(encode(T)) == T
- Cytoscape: validate JSON schema matches Cytoscape.js spec
- pandas adjacency: symmetric for undirected, verify weight preservation
- NX cross-validation on all functions with small test graphs","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-22T17:34:38.243649733Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-22T22:43:05.279391091Z","closed_at":"2026-03-22T22:43:05.278868763Z","close_reason":"Implemented 7 conversion functions: from/to_pandas_adjacency, from/to_prufer_sequence, from/to_nested_tuple, attr_sparse_matrix.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conversion"]}
